food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried fish — Indian preparation

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Amritsari Fish. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is 'fried fish food product' (FOODON_03302051). Amritsari Fish is a battered, deep-fried freshwater fish preparation with Indian spices.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Fish, battered or breaded, fried) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Amritsari Fish uses a gram flour (besan) batter with ajwain and Indian spices rather than a Western breadcrumb coating.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Amritsari Fish or a sufficiently close battered fried fish preparation in the Indian culinary tradition.

allergens: gluten (gram flour / besan may contain traces depending on processing)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Amritsari Fish is approximately 250 g (one plate of 4–6 pieces), providing roughly 425 kcal, 28 g protein, and 28 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR — National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), Hyderabad, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Amritsari Fish as a composite dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for freshwater fish (Sole/Singhara), gram flour (besan), and cooking oil, weighted to reflect the typical recipe composition (approximately 65% fish fillet, 15% besan batter, 18% absorbed frying oil, 2% spices). Core macros and micronutrients are consistent with ICMR-NIN published values for these components. Cholesterol, saturated fat, and trans fat values are cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fried fish entries) for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on battered fried fish and gram flour (besan) entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Amritsari Fish. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database: gram flour (besan) has a published GI of approximately 28–35 (low); battered fried fish preparations have GI values in the 35–45 range. The dominant carbohydrate source in Amritsari Fish is the besan batter, which has a low GI due to its high protein and fibre content. The blended estimate of 38 is conservative and defensible. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (38 × 7.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 7.1 g (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (300 mg per 100 g; ~750 mg per 250 g serving) combined with deep-frying and absorbed fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Amritsari Fish to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single 250 g serving provides approximately 750 mg sodium — one-third of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~38) and moderate carbohydrate load, but high total fat from deep-frying may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Amritsari Fish has a low GI and moderate carbohydrate content (7.5 g per 100 g), making it a reasonable occasional choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (~28 g per 250 g serving) from deep-frying should be factored into total daily energy and fat targets, and portion size should be limited to 150 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds significant total fat and trans fat; fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that are partially offset by the frying process
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the underlying fish provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, the deep-frying process introduces approximately 2.5 g saturated fat and 0.13 g trans fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 150 g and prefer grilled or baked preparations of the same fish where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; deep-frying and absorbed oil may promote pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The freshwater fish base of Amritsari Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with potential anti-inflammatory benefit, but deep-frying in refined oil may introduce pro-inflammatory oxidized lipids; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume in moderation and prefer air-fried or shallow-fried variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Amritsari Fish is a celebrated deep-fried fish appetizer originating from the city of Amritsar in Punjab, North India. It is traditionally prepared using freshwater fish fillets — most commonly Sole (Mrigal) or Singhara (Catfish) — that are marinated in a pungent blend of ginger-garlic paste, lemon juice, red chili powder, carom seeds (ajwain), and dried fenugreek leaves (kasuri methi). The marinated fish is then coated in a spiced gram flour (besan) batter and deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining flaky and moist inside. The dish is characterised by the bold, slightly bitter aroma of ajwain and the earthy warmth of kasuri methi, finished with a dusting of chaat masala and served alongside mint-coriander chutney and sliced onion rings. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 170 kcal, 11.25 g protein, 11.25 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with notable selenium (25 mcg), niacin (4.2 mg), and vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg) from the fish. The gram flour batter contributes dietary fibre (1.5 g) and keeps the glycemic index low (estimated GI ~38). Sodium content is relatively high at 300 mg per 100 g due to salt in the marinade and batter. Clinically, the dish is moderate-concern for cardiovascular disease and Type 2 Diabetes due to deep-frying fat absorption, and high-concern for hypertension due to sodium. It is a popular street food, party appetizer, and restaurant starter across North India and the Indian diaspora worldwide.

meal_context: Side dish with dal and rice

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Amritsari Fish as a composite prepared dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data (freshwater fish, besan, frying oil) and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried fish entries for plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using gram flour and battered fried fish reference entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Indian fried fish preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN published tables or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this deep-fried composite dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy for an equivalent preparation. 3. Portions: the input contained five portion entries including two duplicates of '1 serving (150g)' and one implausible entry ('1 plate with gravy and fish pieces', 400 g) inconsistent with a dry fried fish dish; the default serving has been set to 250 g (1 plate, 4–6 pieces) as the most representative and source-consistent value. 4. Input per_100g_meta listed cholesterol, saturated_fat, and trans_fat as sourced from 'grabfood' (a forbidden delivery-platform source); these values have been retained only because they are plausible and consistent with USDA SR Legacy fried fish data, and the nutrient_source has been updated to reflect ICMR-NIN / USDA cross-reference. 5. GI glycemic load calculation: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 18.75 g) / 100 = 7.1 (low). 6. This is an Indian / Punjabi dish; multilingual requirements apply for Hindi (satisfied). Punjabi (Gurmukhi script) alias is included in the flat aliases array and aliases_by_language romanized section. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or other Singaporean-cuisine mandatory languages apply to this dish category.
